title: Yorktown: The Battle That Ended an Empire
subtitle: October 1781 — The Siege That Created a Nation
tagline: Britain had the most powerful navy on earth. France put it in the wrong place at the wrong time. A French admiral nobody remembers won America's independence.
viral_hook: America's most famous battle wasn't won by Washington — it was won by a French admiral at sea, three hundred miles away, that nobody's ever heard of. Without de Grasse, there is no Yorktown. Without Yorktown, there is no United States.
youtube_title: Battle of Yorktown 1781: The Siege That Ended British America
lesson: Victory often comes from the unexpected theater — the side battle that decides the main battle.

scenes.title: Six Years of War
scenes.narration: October 1781. The American Revolutionary War has been fought for six years. The Continental Army has survived Valley Forge, the betrayal of Benedict Arnold, the loss of Charleston, and the near-defeat at Camden. Britain controls New York, controls the seas, and has just launched a new campaign in the South under the aggressive Lord Cornwallis. But Cornwallis has driven his army up through the Carolinas and into Virginia, and now — for reasons his superiors in New York find difficult to explain — he has marched his army to the small tobacco port of Yorktown on the Virginia coast and ordered his men to dig in. He is waiting for reinforcement or evacuation by sea. The British navy controls the sea. He has no reason to be afraid. He is about to lose.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. October 1781: six years of American revolution. The Continental Army — battered but unbroken. Lord Cornwallis and his British army at Yorktown, Virginia — digging in, waiting for the navy. The small tobacco port on the York River. Cornwallis with no reason to fear, about to face the end. 16:9.

scenes.title: The American Revolution — Six Years In
scenes.narration: By 1781, the American Revolutionary War had ground into exhausting attrition. The Continental Army under Washington had failed to defeat the British in a major battle since the early victories of 1776-77. Britain's strategy of controlling major port cities — Boston, New York, Philadelphia, Charleston — while ravaging the countryside was grinding down American civilian support. France had entered the war in 1778, Spain in 1779, providing money, weapons, and naval power. But French naval operations had repeatedly failed to achieve decisive results. Washington desperately wanted to attack New York. His French allies had a different target in mind. The convergence of these plans on a small Virginia port would end the war.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The American Revolution by 1781: a map showing British control of ports, the Continental Army in the field, French intervention since 1778. Washington's frustration — years of war without a decisive blow. The French alliance: money, weapons, ships. The strategic question: New York or somewhere else? The answer coming together. 16:9.

scenes.title: Washington and Rochambeau
scenes.narration: George Washington had been commanding the Continental Army since 1775. He was fifty years old, still fighting a war that had consumed his prime years. He was a military commander of uneven battlefield record and extraordinary moral authority — the man who kept the army together through defeats that would have broken any other commander. His French counterpart was Lieutenant General the Comte de Rochambeau, who commanded six thousand French regular troops in Newport, Rhode Island. Rochambeau was a professional soldier, carefully competent, who handled Washington with the delicacy required when advising an ally who was technically the senior partner. Together, they would make the plan that ended the war.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Washington and Rochambeau — two commanders who needed each other. Washington: tall, commanding, burdened by six years of war and the weight of a revolution. Rochambeau: professional, careful, the French regular who would supply what Washington lacked. The alliance that would produce Yorktown. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Man Who Made It Possible — de Grasse
scenes.narration: Admiral François-Joseph Paul, Comte de Grasse, is the least famous decisive figure in the American Revolution. He commanded the French fleet in the West Indies — twenty-eight ships of the line, the most powerful French naval force in the western hemisphere. In August 1781, de Grasse received word from Rochambeau requesting he bring his fleet north to the Chesapeake Bay to support an operation against Cornwallis. De Grasse was not obligated to come. He was fighting his own Caribbean campaign. He had Dutch islands to manage and British fleets to watch. He decided to come anyway, bringing his entire fleet — a decision that would be called the most consequential naval act of the eighteenth century.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Admiral de Grasse — the French fleet commander who made Yorktown possible. His fleet in the Caribbean: 28 ships of the line. The message from Rochambeau arriving. De Grasse's decision: bring the entire fleet north. The man nobody taught you in school who made independence possible. His face: a decision being made that will change history. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Plan Comes Together
scenes.narration: In August 1781, Washington and Rochambeau began a deception operation that is still studied in military schools. They marched their combined armies south from New York as if preparing to attack the British there — fake camp layouts, fake supply depots, fake orders designed to make Clinton in New York believe they were preparing for an assault on the city. Then, in a march of nearly five hundred miles in thirty days, Washington and Rochambeau's armies wheeled south toward Virginia. Clinton in New York didn't understand what was happening until the allied army had already passed Philadelphia. By then, de Grasse's fleet was in the Chesapeake. The trap was set.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The deception: fake camps and supply depots outside New York fooling Clinton. Then the five-hundred mile march south — the combined American and French armies wheeling away from New York toward Virginia. The speed: 500 miles in 30 days. Clinton in New York finally understanding what's happening, too late. De Grasse's fleet already in the Chesapeake. The trap closing. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Battle of the Chesapeake
scenes.narration: On September 5, 1781, the British fleet under Admiral Thomas Graves arrived at the mouth of the Chesapeake Bay to relieve Cornwallis. What they found was de Grasse's fleet waiting for them — nineteen British ships against twenty-four French. The Battle of the Chesapeake lasted two hours and was tactically indecisive in the traditional sense — neither fleet was destroyed. But strategically, it was the most decisive naval battle of the age. Graves retreated to New York to repair his damaged ships. De Grasse held the Chesapeake. Cornwallis was trapped — no British ship could reach him. The army at Yorktown had no escape by sea. The battle nobody fought to a conclusion had decided the outcome before the siege began.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Battle of the Chesapeake: British and French fleets engaging at the mouth of the bay. The tactical indecision — neither fleet destroyed. Then the strategic verdict: Graves retreating to New York, de Grasse holding the Chesapeake. Cornwallis cut off. The naval battle that decided Yorktown without either side winning it outright. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Siege Begins
scenes.narration: With de Grasse controlling the Chesapeake, Washington and Rochambeau arrived at Yorktown in late September with seventeen thousand Allied troops — nine thousand American, eight thousand French — to face Cornwallis's eight thousand British and Hessian defenders. Cornwallis was outnumbered more than two to one, trapped on a peninsula with no sea route of escape, and knew it. He sent word to Clinton in New York requesting immediate rescue. Clinton promised to come. The relief fleet was not ready. Washington began formal siege operations on October 6, 1781. Parallel trenches were dug toward the British lines in the classic siege method of the era — a slow, methodical, mathematical approach to reducing a fortified position.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The siege of Yorktown beginning: 17,000 Allied troops surrounding 8,000 British defenders on a peninsula. The formal siege works — parallel trenches advancing toward the British lines. Cornwallis sending urgent messages to Clinton. Clinton promising relief. The relief fleet not ready. The mathematical certainty of the siege closing in. 16:9.

scenes.title: Storming the Redoubts
scenes.narration: On the night of October 14, two Allied columns stormed the final British defensive positions — Redoubt 9 and Redoubt 10. The American column, led by Lieutenant Colonel Alexander Hamilton — yes, that Alexander Hamilton — attacked Redoubt 10 with fixed bayonets, no muskets loaded, on Washington's direct orders. The attack had to be silent. Hamilton's men scrambled over the palisades under fire and took the redoubt in ten minutes. Simultaneously, French troops took Redoubt 9. With both redoubts taken, the allies could complete their third parallel — the final siege line that would bring their cannon close enough to destroy what remained of the British defenses. The end was now a matter of days.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The night assault on the redoubts: Hamilton's men charging Redoubt 10 in darkness with fixed bayonets — silent, fast, terrifying. The ten-minute fight. French troops taking Redoubt 9 simultaneously. The two redoubts taken: the siege in its final phase. Hamilton — young, brilliant, furious with energy — the face of the night's victory. 16:9.

scenes.title: Cornwallis's Last Gamble
scenes.narration: On the night of October 16, Cornwallis attempted a desperate escape. He began moving his army across the York River to Gloucester Point, where a small British garrison held the north bank — hoping to break out overland and march north to escape the siege. A storm on the river scattered his boats, returned his men to Yorktown, and ended the attempt. On the morning of October 17, Cornwallis's artillery fired its final shots. The cannon were down to their last rounds. A drummer and an officer appeared on the British parapet waving a white flag. After two weeks of siege, the army that had won battle after battle through the Carolinas was surrendering.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Cornwallis's night escape attempt: British boats on the York River in the dark, a storm scattering them. The men returned to Yorktown. The last cannon fire on the morning of October 17. Then: a British drummer boy on the parapet, a white flag in the morning light. The surrender signal after two weeks of siege. 16:9.

scenes.title: The World Turned Upside Down
scenes.narration: October 19, 1781. The British army at Yorktown marched out to surrender. Eight thousand soldiers, their flags cased, their weapons to be stacked. The British band played a tune called 'The World Turned Upside Down' — a traditional song about reversals of order, about things that couldn't happen happening. Cornwallis claimed illness and sent a subordinate, General O'Hara, to surrender his sword. Washington, insulted by the snub, sent his own subordinate, General Lincoln, to receive it. The ceremony was formal, bitter, and strange. The army that represented the most powerful empire on earth was laying down its arms to farmers, merchants, lawyers, and their French allies. The world had, in fact, been turned upside down.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The British surrender at Yorktown: the army marching out, flags cased, to stack their weapons. The British band playing 'The World Turned Upside Down.' General O'Hara surrendering to General Lincoln while Cornwallis and Washington stay away. The formal, bitter ceremony. Eight thousand British soldiers surrendering to the nation they said could never exist. 16:9.

scenes.title: London Reacts
scenes.narration: When news of Yorktown reached London in November 1781, Prime Minister Lord North reportedly said 'Oh God, it is all over.' He was right. The British government did not immediately recognize American independence, and the war technically continued at sea and in the colonies. But Yorktown broke the political will to continue fighting. The costs of the war — financial, military, diplomatic — had been enormous, and now the largest army in America had surrendered. Parliamentary opposition to the war intensified. North's government fell in March 1782. Peace negotiations began. The Treaty of Paris, recognizing American independence and granting America territory to the Mississippi River, was signed in September 1783.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. London learning of Yorktown: Lord North's reaction — 'Oh God, it is all over.' The parliamentary debate turning against the war. North's government falling. The peace negotiations beginning. The Treaty of Paris signed: American independence recognized, the territory to the Mississippi River granted. The British empire's loss creating a new nation. 16:9.

scenes.title: The French Alliance
scenes.narration: The decisive role of France at Yorktown is often underemphasized in American accounts of the Revolution. De Grasse's fleet made the siege possible by sealing the Chesapeake. Rochambeau's eight thousand French regulars made up nearly half the besieging army. French money — forty million livres lent to the Continental Congress — had kept the American war effort funded when Congress was bankrupt. French weapons had equipped the Continental Army. Without France, the American Revolution would very likely have failed. France's reasons for helping were purely strategic — revenge against Britain for the Seven Years' War — not ideological sympathy for republicanism. But the alliance worked, and the result was a nation.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The French contribution at Yorktown: de Grasse's fleet, Rochambeau's army, French money and weapons. The French flags alongside American flags at the siege. The strategic calculation behind the alliance — revenge for the Seven Years' War. The irony: a monarchy funding a republic to weaken another monarchy. Without France, no Yorktown. Without Yorktown, no United States. 16:9.

scenes.title: Alexander Hamilton at Yorktown
scenes.narration: Alexander Hamilton had served as Washington's aide-de-camp for four years, handling correspondence and managing the general's communications while chafing at his desk role. He had repeatedly requested a field command and been denied. At Yorktown, Washington gave him his chance — command of the assault on Redoubt 10. Hamilton's attack was textbook: fast, silent, controlled. The redoubt was taken in ten minutes with minimal casualties. Hamilton was twenty-six years old. He would go on to become the first Secretary of the Treasury, the architect of American financial policy, the author of the Federalist Papers alongside Madison and Jay, and the man who died at age forty-nine in a duel with Aaron Burr.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Alexander Hamilton at Yorktown: the young officer leading the assault on Redoubt 10 — his moment of military glory after four years at a desk. Then the arc forward: Hamilton as Treasury Secretary, the Federalist Papers, the duel with Burr. The man who charged a British redoubt in 1781 shaping the nation's finances for decades afterward. 16:9.

scenes.title: Washington's War
scenes.narration: George Washington had commanded the Continental Army for six and a half years. He had won battles and lost battles. He had kept an army together through defeats, desertions, lack of pay, lack of food, lack of boots in winter. His strategy was ultimately not to win the war militarily but to outlast Britain's willingness to fight it — to survive until the political cost of the war outweighed the political cost of conceding. Yorktown was the capstone: the decisive military victory that made the political concession unavoidable. Washington had managed his army, his allies, his Congress, and his own temperament through years of adversity. Yorktown was the moment when all of it paid.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Washington's six-year arc: Valley Forge and the frozen army, the defeats and the retreats, the men who stayed and the men who left. Then Yorktown: the siege, the surrender, the fulfillment. Washington watching the British march out to stack their weapons. Six and a half years compressed into one morning of victory. 16:9.

scenes.title: Why Britain Lost
scenes.narration: Britain lost the American Revolution for reasons that go beyond any single battle. The fundamental problem was strategic: Britain had to pacify and hold a continent three thousand miles away with a professional army too small for the task, against a population that could absorb indefinite occupation but never be fully controlled. The Loyalist strategy — using American supporters of the Crown to restore order — failed because Loyalists were always a minority and were exposed to retaliation when British troops moved on. The French alliance gave the Americans the naval power to make one moment decisive. That moment was Yorktown. But Yorktown was possible because Britain's strategic position was already untenable.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Britain's strategic problem visualized: holding a continent 3,000 miles away, too few troops, a population that would not yield. The Loyalist failure. The French intervention tipping the balance. Yorktown as the moment when an already-failing strategy became obviously, undeniably finished. 16:9.

scenes.title: What Yorktown Made
scenes.narration: The Treaty of Paris gave the United States independence and territory extending to the Mississippi River — twice the size of the original thirteen colonies. From that base, the United States expanded to the Pacific Ocean, absorbed Louisiana from Napoleon in 1803, purchased Florida, annexed Texas, and fought a war with Mexico that added California and the Southwest. The nation that grew from the Yorktown settlement became, within a century and a half, the world's largest economy and the dominant military power on earth. The siege of a small Virginia tobacco port in 1781 set in motion a chain of events whose consequences are still unfolding.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Treaty of Paris and its gift: the United States to the Mississippi River. Then the expansion westward — Louisiana Purchase, Florida, Texas, California — a nation growing across a continent from the seed of a 1781 siege. The small tobacco port at Yorktown and the superpower it made possible. 16:9.

scenes.title: Yorktown Today
scenes.narration: Yorktown, Virginia, is a small town on the York River with a population of a few hundred people. The siege works are preserved — the original trenches, the redoubts that Hamilton stormed, the field where the British surrendered. The Yorktown Victory Monument stands on the bluff above the river. Tourists walk the same ground where eight thousand British soldiers stacked their muskets on October 19, 1781. The grass is green. The river is quiet. It looks like nothing. It was everything — the field where a nation was confirmed, where the war ended, where the world turned upside down and stayed that way.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Yorktown today: the small town on the York River, the preserved siege works and redoubts. The Yorktown Victory Monument. Tourists walking the surrender field. The ground where Britain's army stacked their weapons in 1781. The quiet, green field that was the hinge of American history. 16:9.

scenes.title: Cornwallis After Yorktown
scenes.narration: Lord Cornwallis recovered from the humiliation of Yorktown to have a distinguished subsequent career — he was not held responsible in Britain for the loss, as the fault was attributed to Clinton's failure to relieve him in time. He served as Governor-General of India, where he implemented legal reforms still called the Cornwallis Code. He served as Viceroy of Ireland. He negotiated the Treaty of Amiens with Napoleon. He died in India in 1805. The general who surrendered at Yorktown is remembered in Britain not primarily for that loss but for his subsequent accomplishments. History is complicated that way.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Cornwallis's career after Yorktown: the failure not blamed on him, his appointments as Governor-General of India and Viceroy of Ireland. The Cornwallis Code of legal reforms in India. The treaty with Napoleon. His death in India in 1805. The man who lost America, honored elsewhere. The irony of reputations. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Decisive Theater
scenes.narration: Yorktown teaches that wars are often decided in unexpected theaters. Washington wanted to attack New York — the most obvious objective, the British stronghold. Rochambeau and de Grasse saw Cornwallis's exposed position in Virginia as a trap that could be sprung. The decisive blow of the Revolution was not struck at the most defended place but at the most exposed place, by a convergence of forces that required cooperation between an American general, a French general, and a French admiral who might never have sailed north at all. The lesson: where the enemy is most vulnerable, not where they are most powerful, is where decisive action is possible.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The unexpected theater: not New York but Virginia. Not the strongest British position but the most exposed. The three-way convergence — Washington, Rochambeau, de Grasse — each necessary, none sufficient alone. The decisive blow struck where the enemy didn't expect it. The lesson in the map. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Continental Army's Last Year
scenes.narration: The soldiers of the Continental Army who marched to Yorktown were the survivors of a six-year war. Many had served since 1775. They had been unpaid for months. They had mutinied twice — the Pennsylvania Line in January 1781, the New Jersey Line the same month — before returning to duty. They had marched five hundred miles south in the final weeks. When the British marched out to surrender, these men had won something that six years earlier seemed impossible: independence from the most powerful empire in the world. Some of them wept. Most were too tired to feel much at all. They still had to march home.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Continental Army soldiers at Yorktown — the survivors of six years of war. Tired, unpaid, far from home. Watching the British march out to surrender. Some of them weeping. Most just standing, too exhausted to celebrate. They had done the impossible. Now they had to march home. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Revolution's Promises
scenes.narration: The American Revolution promised life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness to all men — and immediately failed to deliver on that promise for hundreds of thousands of enslaved people. The Constitution would perpetuate slavery. The Declaration's language was written by a man who held over six hundred enslaved people in his lifetime. The Revolution's unfinished business — the contradiction between its ideals and its practice — would define American history for the next two centuries and beyond. Yorktown was a genuine triumph. The republic it created was genuine and flawed in equal measure. Both things are true.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Revolution's contradiction: the Declaration's promise of liberty alongside the reality of slavery. Jefferson writing 'all men are created equal' while enslaved people worked his plantation. The founding ideals and the founding failures in the same frame. The republic that was both genuinely revolutionary and genuinely incomplete. 16:9.

scenes.title: What the Revolution Proved
scenes.narration: The American Revolution proved that a colonial population could defeat an imperial power through sustained irregular resistance, external alliance, and the political will to outlast a more powerful enemy. It proved that professional European armies could be fought to a stalemate by citizen soldiers when motivation, terrain, and strategy were aligned. It proved that the French alliance — strategic, not ideological — could provide the margin of victory at the decisive moment. And it proved that the idea of a republic governed by its citizens, novel and fragile in 1776, could survive the test of war and emerge as a functioning government. These were not small things to prove.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. What the Revolution proved: colonial resistance defeating empire, the French alliance providing the margin, the republic surviving the test of war. The Continental Army and the French fleet and the idea of self-government, all validated at Yorktown. The proof of concept for what became the modern world's dominant political form. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Day the World Turned
scenes.narration: On October 19, 1781, at a small tobacco port in Virginia, the British army that had fought for six years to hold a continent laid down its weapons to a nation that had refused to stop fighting. An admiral nobody remembers sealed the Chesapeake. Two generals from different nations marched five hundred miles together. A twenty-six-year-old future Secretary of the Treasury took a redoubt in ten minutes with fixed bayonets. And a nation was confirmed that had been only a declaration five years before. The world turned upside down that morning. It never turned back.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The morning of October 19, 1781: the British army marching out at Yorktown. The stacked weapons. The new nation confirmed. The French and American flags side by side. De Grasse's fleet in the Chesapeake. Hamilton's redoubt taken. Washington watching. The world that was made that morning. 16:9.

scenes.title: History's Hinge Points
scenes.narration: Subscribe to Gods and Glory — the battles that made the world, told with the depth they deserve. Season 3 complete. Thank you for watching every episode. The next battle is already being prepared.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Final image: the Yorktown surrender field at dawn — the quiet ground where history turned. The sun coming up on a new nation. Gods and Glory channel logo. Subscribe prompt. Season 3 complete. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Revolution — Six Years of Exhaustion
scenes.narration: By 1781 the American Revolution had been grinding for six years. The Continental Army had survived Valley Forge, survived the loss of Philadelphia, survived the treason of Benedict Arnold. But survival was not victory. Washington had never won a decisive battle. The British held New York, Charleston, and Savannah. American finances were collapsing — Congress couldn't pay its soldiers, some units had mutinied. France had been an ally since 1778 but the alliance had produced limited results. The war appeared to be settling into a stalemate the Americans couldn't sustain.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Continental Army in 1781 after six years of war — ragged soldiers, unpaid for months, the Revolution surviving but not winning, the stalemate that threatened to outlast American will. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Cornwallis — The British Commander in the South
scenes.narration: General Charles Cornwallis had spent 1780-1781 conducting a brutal and ultimately pointless campaign through the Carolinas. He had won battles at Camden and Guilford Courthouse, but winning battles while losing the strategic war. The Patriot forces he defeated reformed. His victories cost men he couldn't replace. In the spring of 1781 he moved to Virginia, the colony he believed was the key to ending the rebellion. He had 7,500 soldiers, the largest British force in America. He was about to be caught in a trap he didn't know was closing.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. General Cornwallis in Virginia in 1781 — the British commander who won battles in the Carolinas but lost the campaign, now moving to Virginia with 7,500 soldiers. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Yorktown — Why Cornwallis Stopped There
scenes.narration: Cornwallis chose Yorktown as his base at the instruction of British Commander-in-Chief Henry Clinton in New York. Clinton wanted a deep-water port where the Royal Navy could supply and reinforce his forces. Yorktown sat on a peninsula between the York and James Rivers — defensible, with good anchorage. It was also a trap: the only overland exit was a narrow neck of land, and the sea approaches were controlled by whoever had naval superiority. Cornwallis dug in at Yorktown in August 1781, unaware that a French fleet was sailing for the Chesapeake.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Yorktown peninsula in 1781 — the defensive position between the York and James Rivers that Clinton ordered Cornwallis to hold, the geography that would become a trap. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: De Grasse — The Admiral Who Made It Possible
scenes.narration: Admiral François de Grasse commanded the French West Indies fleet — 28 ships of the line, the most powerful French naval force in the western hemisphere. In August 1781 he received a message from Washington and Rochambeau: could he bring his fleet to the Chesapeake? De Grasse made the most consequential naval decision of the American Revolution: he would bring his entire fleet, 3,000 French soldiers, and enough supplies to sustain a siege. He would sail in August and could remain until mid-October. The window was fixed. Washington and Rochambeau had to move immediately.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Admiral de Grasse reviewing his fleet of 28 ships in the Caribbean — the French admiral whose decision to sail the entire fleet to the Chesapeake made the Yorktown campaign possible. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The March South — The Deception
scenes.narration: Washington needed to move his army from New York to Virginia — 450 miles — without the British in New York understanding what was happening. He ran an elaborate deception: fake letters were allowed to be captured suggesting an attack on New York, camps were built in New Jersey to suggest a siege, army units moved with deliberate visibility near Manhattan. British Commander Clinton watched the preparations for an attack that was never coming while Washington's army marched south. By the time Clinton realized what was happening, the French and American forces were in Virginia.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Washington's army marching south from New York while fake camps and captured letters deceive Clinton — the strategic deception that moved 9,000 men 450 miles without British interference. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Battle of the Chesapeake — September 5
scenes.narration: On September 5, 1781, the British fleet under Admiral Graves met de Grasse's French fleet outside the Chesapeake Bay. The Battle of the Chesapeake was tactically inconclusive — neither side lost a ship. But strategically it was decisive. Graves failed to break through to relieve Cornwallis. The French fleet controlled the Chesapeake. When Graves sailed back to New York for repairs, Cornwallis was sealed in Yorktown. The battle that decided the American Revolution was not at Yorktown — it was at sea on September 5, five weeks before Cornwallis surrendered.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Battle of the Chesapeake on September 5, 1781 — British and French fleets exchanging fire outside the bay, the inconclusive action that sealed Cornwallis's fate by leaving France in control of the water. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Siege Begins — October 6
scenes.narration: The Franco-American force that arrived at Yorktown numbered approximately 17,000 — nearly twice Cornwallis's 7,500. Washington formally opened siege operations on October 6. The approach trenches were dug by 1,500 men working through the night — Washington himself reportedly turned the first shovelful of earth. By October 9, the first Allied batteries were in place. Washington fired the first American cannon. By October 10, Cornwallis's outer defenses were being systematically destroyed. The British had no cavalry to disrupt the siege works. The trap was closing.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Yorktown siege opening on October 6 — 1,500 men digging approach trenches overnight, Washington turning the first earth, the Allied batteries opening fire on October 9. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Redoubts 9 and 10 — The Night Attack
scenes.narration: The key to Yorktown was two British earthwork fortifications — Redoubt 9 and Redoubt 10 — that flanked the British line. On the night of October 14, two columns attacked simultaneously. Alexander Hamilton led the American assault on Redoubt 10 with unloaded muskets — bayonets only, no shots to alert defenders until they were over the wall. The French took Redoubt 9 in a parallel attack. Both redoubts were taken in under 10 minutes. With the redoubts in Allied hands, the siege guns could advance to decisive range. Cornwallis's position was now hopeless.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Alexander Hamilton's column scaling Redoubt 10 at night with bayonets only — the simultaneous attacks on Redoubts 9 and 10 that broke the British defensive line at Yorktown. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Cornwallis's Options — All Bad
scenes.narration: After October 14, Cornwallis had three options. He could counterattack the Allied siege works — attempted on October 16 with 350 men, partially successful, ultimately reversed. He could attempt to cross the York River to Gloucester Point and escape north — attempted on October 16 with boats, storms scattered the crossing, he turned back. He could wait for Clinton's promised relief fleet from New York — it arrived October 24, six days after the surrender. Every option failed or arrived too late. On October 17, Cornwallis sent a message requesting a ceasefire to discuss terms.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Cornwallis's shrinking options in mid-October 1781 — the failed counterattack, the storm-scattered river crossing, the relief fleet that arrived too late, the converging catastrophe. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Surrender — October 19, 1781
scenes.narration: The surrender ceremony was set for October 19, 1781. Cornwallis claimed illness and sent Brigadier General O'Hara in his place. O'Hara first approached Rochambeau to surrender — the French commander pointed him to Washington. Washington refused to accept the surrender from a subordinate and directed O'Hara to his own second, General Lincoln. The British band played a song called 'The World Turned Upside Down.' 7,087 British soldiers marched out and stacked their arms. The officer who commanded them reportedly avoided looking at the Allied lines, staring straight ahead as his army ended the American Revolution.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Yorktown surrender ceremony October 19, 1781 — British soldiers marching out to stack their arms, the band playing The World Turned Upside Down, the moment the American Revolution was won. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The World Turned Upside Down
scenes.narration: The song the British band played at the Yorktown surrender — 'The World Turned Upside Down' — was an English ballad about disorder and impossibility. Its selection was either deeply ironic or deeply appropriate. A colonial militia army that had been losing for six years, funded by a bankrupted congress, fed by farmers and supported by a European ally, had defeated the most powerful military force on earth. The world had, in fact, turned upside down. The song the British chose to surrender to became the most appropriate epitaph for the most consequential military defeat in British history.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. British soldiers stacking arms at Yorktown while their band plays The World Turned Upside Down — the surrender ceremony that ended the American Revolution, the world literally changing as they marched. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Clinton's Relief Fleet — Six Days Late
scenes.narration: Admiral Graves's relief fleet from New York arrived off the Chesapeake on October 24 — six days after the surrender. It carried 7,000 reinforcements that would have outnumbered the besieging Allied force if they had arrived before the capitulation. Graves had been delayed by slow repairs after the September 5 battle. Clinton had delayed sending the fleet by weeks of indecision. The margin of victory at Yorktown was six days. The American Revolution was decided by the difference between de Grasse arriving on time and Graves arriving late.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The British relief fleet arriving off the Chesapeake on October 24 — six days too late, 7,000 reinforcements that could have changed everything, the margin of the American Revolution's outcome. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Why Britain Lost — The Strategic Failure
scenes.narration: Britain lost the American Revolution not at Yorktown but through seven years of strategic failure. They never committed enough force consistently. They couldn't suppress rebellion in the north while fighting in the south. They couldn't maintain naval superiority across the Atlantic against France, Spain, and Holland simultaneously. They underestimated American resilience and French commitment. Yorktown was the military conclusion of a political failure: a government in London that never agreed on war aims, alternating between suppression and conciliation, never fully committing to either. The world's greatest empire lost thirteen colonies because it never decided how hard to try to keep them.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. British strategic failures mapped across seven years — the divided government, the insufficient forces, the naval overextension, the underestimation of American will, the structural reasons Britain lost the Revolution. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Treaty of Paris — What Yorktown Won
scenes.narration: The Treaty of Paris, signed September 3, 1783, gave the United States everything it had fought for and more. Britain recognized American independence and ceded territory to the Mississippi River — far beyond what the original thirteen colonies had occupied. The United States received fishing rights off Newfoundland. The boundary with Canada was settled. Britain received only the promise that Congress would 'recommend' states treat loyalists fairly — a promise that was largely ignored. The treaty that ended the Revolution was extraordinarily generous to the Americans because Yorktown had made refusal impossible.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Treaty of Paris 1783 — the map of American territory extending to the Mississippi River, the diplomatic victory that matched the military one at Yorktown. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Washington and Power — The General Who Went Home
scenes.narration: After Yorktown, Washington's officers proposed he become king. He refused with contempt. When he resigned his commission in December 1783, he did so before Congress in Annapolis, physically handing back the authority he had been given. The Roman general Cincinnatus had returned to his farm after saving Rome — Washington explicitly modeled himself on Cincinnatus. King George III, told that Washington would resign and return to his farm, reportedly said: 'If he does that, he will be the greatest man in the world.' He did. He was. The general who could have been king chose to be a citizen instead.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Washington resigning his commission before Congress in Annapolis in December 1783 — the general who refused a crown handing back his authority, the most consequential act of political self-denial in American history. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: October 19, 1781 — When the World Changed
scenes.narration: On October 19, 1781, 7,087 British soldiers marched out of Yorktown and stacked their arms while their band played about a world turned upside down. It was. The colonies that had been British territory six years earlier were now a nation. The republic that would grow to become the most powerful state in human history was born in that moment — born from six years of desperate fighting, French money and ships, a march of 450 miles, a naval battle on September 5 that most people have never heard of, and two redoubts taken at bayonet point on October 14. The world the British band described had turned. It has not turned back.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Yorktown at dusk on October 19, 1781 — British soldiers stacking arms, Washington watching, the surrender that created the United States, the moment the modern world began to take its current shape. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Lafayette — The French Connection
scenes.narration: The Marquis de Lafayette had been fighting with Washington since 1777, aged 19. By 1781 he commanded American forces in Virginia tasked with tracking Cornwallis. Too small to fight, he shadowed the British army and kept Washington informed of Cornwallis's movements. His intelligence reports were critical to Washington's planning. When Cornwallis settled at Yorktown, Lafayette tightened his cordon to prevent escape. The French aristocrat who had come to America for adventure at 19 was instrumental in trapping Cornwallis at 24. Lafayette's contribution to Yorktown is typically underweighted in American accounts.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Lafayette commanding American forces tracking Cornwallis through Virginia in 1781 — the 24-year-old French aristocrat whose intelligence reports and cordon helped trap the British at Yorktown. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Siege Guns — French Artillery
scenes.narration: The artillery that destroyed Cornwallis's defenses at Yorktown was predominantly French. Washington's Continental Army had been chronically short of heavy guns throughout the war. The French siege train brought to Yorktown included guns that could fire accurately at 1,000 yards — far beyond British return fire. French gunners were among the best in the world, trained in the scientific artillery tradition of the French army. When Washington fired the first cannon on October 9, he was firing a French gun. The firepower that forced Cornwallis to surrender was largely a French contribution.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. French siege guns positioned at Yorktown — the heavy artillery that Washington's Continental Army could never have assembled alone, the French firepower that made the siege decisive. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Hamilton's Charge — The Future Secretary's Last Battle
scenes.narration: Alexander Hamilton had served as Washington's aide-de-camp for four years, repeatedly requesting a combat command. Washington kept refusing — Hamilton was too valuable as a staff officer. At Yorktown, Hamilton finally got his battle. He led the assault on Redoubt 10 on the night of October 14 with unloaded muskets and bayonets. The redoubt was taken in ten minutes. It was Hamilton's only major combat command. The man who would write the Federalist Papers, serve as the first Treasury Secretary, and shape American economic policy won his military reputation in ten minutes at Yorktown with a bayonet.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Alexander Hamilton leading the bayonet assault on Redoubt 10 at Yorktown — the future Treasury Secretary in his only major combat command, taking the fortification in ten minutes. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Cornwallis's Illness — Real or Convenient
scenes.narration: Cornwallis claimed he was too ill to attend the surrender ceremony on October 19, sending Brigadier General O'Hara in his place. Whether the illness was genuine or diplomatic is debated by historians. Attending a surrender ceremony was the most humiliating experience an 18th century officer could undergo. Sending a subordinate was a recognized face-saving convention. O'Hara then compounded the insult by first approaching Rochambeau — implying Britain preferred to surrender to France than to its former colonies. Washington's response — refusing to deal with a subordinate and directing O'Hara to his own second — was a masterpiece of dignified protocol.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. General O'Hara approaching the surrender ceremony instead of Cornwallis — the British second-in-command attempting to surrender to Rochambeau, Washington's dignified redirect, the protocol of humiliation. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Loyalists — The War's Forgotten Losers
scenes.narration: Approximately one in five American colonists remained loyal to Britain during the Revolution. After Yorktown, approximately 60,000 Loyalists fled to Canada, Britain, or the Caribbean rather than live under the new republic. Their property was confiscated in most states. Families were torn apart. Communities that had been divided by the war remained divided after it. The Treaty of Paris promised Congress would 'recommend' fair treatment for Loyalists — a recommendation almost universally ignored. The American Revolution created America. It also created a refugee crisis of 60,000 people who had chosen the losing side of their own civil war.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Loyalist families departing for Canada after Yorktown — the 60,000 Americans who chose Britain and lost everything, the refugee crisis that followed the Revolution's victory. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: France's Cost — Revolution Imported
scenes.narration: France's support for the American Revolution cost approximately 1.3 billion livres — a sum that pushed France toward bankruptcy. The financial crisis triggered by war debt was a primary cause of the French Revolution of 1789. The soldiers who returned from America brought back ideas about liberty and republican government that combined explosively with French social grievances. Lafayette returned home and became a leader of the early French Revolution. The American Revolution that France helped finance exported its ideas back to France and helped create the revolution that overthrew the French monarchy. Yorktown's consequences reached Paris.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. France in 1789 — the financial crisis triggered by American war debt, Lafayette back home leading the French Revolution, the ideas from America combining with French grievances. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Constitutional Convention — Yorktown's Real Product
scenes.narration: The Articles of Confederation that governed the United States after independence were so weak that Washington called them 'a rope of sand.' The Constitutional Convention of 1787 replaced them with the Constitution — the document that created the actual American government. The men who wrote it were shaped by the Revolution: Washington had seen an army starve for lack of congressional authority to raise funds. Hamilton had seen military decisions paralyzed by thirteen competing state governments. The Constitution was written by men who had experienced the consequences of weak central government at Valley Forge and Morristown. Yorktown made America. The Constitution made it governable.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Constitutional Convention of 1787 — Washington presiding, Hamilton arguing for strong central government, the men shaped by Revolutionary War failures writing the document that made America work. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: What Yorktown Built
scenes.narration: On October 19, 1781, the British Army marched out of Yorktown and the American Revolution was effectively over. What followed was not just a new country but a new idea: that government derives its authority from the consent of the governed, that rights precede government rather than being granted by it, that a written constitution can constrain power. These ideas had existed before America. America made them operational. The republic that stumbled through six years of war, that nearly starved at Valley Forge, that survived on French money and Washington's stubbornness — that republic became the template for every democratic constitution written in the two centuries that followed. Yorktown didn't just end a war. It started something that is still running.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. America after Yorktown — the Constitutional Convention, the new republic's first decades, the template for democratic government that spread across two centuries, the world still shaped by what was decided at a Virginia riverbank. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Continental Army — What Washington Actually Had
scenes.narration: The army Washington commanded at Yorktown was not the army most Americans imagine. The Continental regulars were well-trained by 1781 — Baron von Steuben's work at Valley Forge had transformed them — but they were chronically unpaid, sometimes unfed, and had experienced mutinies as recently as January 1781. The militia forces were unreliable in open battle. Washington's strategic genius throughout the Revolution was recognizing what his army could and couldn't do, and avoiding battles that would expose its weaknesses. At Yorktown, with French regulars beside him and siege operations replacing open battle, he finally had conditions that matched his army's strengths.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Continental Army at Yorktown in 1781 — the well-drilled but chronically underpaid soldiers transformed by von Steuben, finally fighting under conditions that matched their capabilities. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Siege — 18th Century Warfare at Its Most Scientific
scenes.narration: 18th century siege warfare was among the most mathematically precise forms of military operation ever developed. Approach trenches were dug at precise angles to prevent enfilading fire. Parallel trenches were established at calculated distances. Artillery was positioned according to mathematical tables worked out over decades of European warfare. The French engineers who supervised the Yorktown siege were the best in the world — France had developed siege doctrine since Vauban in the 17th century. Cornwallis faced not just an army but a system, refined over a century, that was designed to make every defensive position eventually untenable.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Yorktown siege works — mathematically precise approach trenches and parallel lines, French engineers applying a century of siege doctrine against Cornwallis's earthworks. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Rochambeau — The General Who Took Orders
scenes.narration: General Rochambeau commanded the French Expeditionary Force in America — 7,000 professional soldiers, the best-equipped force in the campaign. He was older than Washington, more experienced, and commanded forces that could arguably have operated independently. Instead, he consistently deferred to Washington's overall command while quietly providing expertise and resources that made Washington's plans executable. At Yorktown, it was Rochambeau who suggested the southern campaign and Rochambeau who facilitated the coordination with de Grasse. The man who made Yorktown possible chose throughout the campaign to be Washington's partner rather than his rival.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. General Rochambeau with Washington at Yorktown — the French commander who deferred to American overall command while quietly providing the expertise and resources that made victory possible. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The American Revolution's Global Context
scenes.narration: The American Revolution was not fought in isolation. Britain was simultaneously at war with France, Spain, and Holland — a global conflict stretching from the Caribbean to India to the English Channel. British resources that might have crushed the American rebellion were diverted to protect Jamaica from France, Gibraltar from Spain, and Bengal from French-allied forces. Yorktown happened partly because Britain couldn't concentrate its navy in American waters while fighting everywhere else simultaneously. The American Revolution succeeded not just because of Valley Forge and Yorktown but because Britain was overextended across the entire globe.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Map of Britain's global wars 1778-1783 — conflicts in the Caribbean, Gibraltar, India, and the Channel simultaneously stretching British resources that could have ended the American rebellion. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Why America Won — Five Real Reasons
scenes.narration: America won the Revolution for five reasons that had little to do with the mythology. First, France's intervention in 1778 made it a world war Britain couldn't afford. Second, Washington's strategic patience avoided the decisive defeat that would have ended the rebellion. Third, the British government never agreed on how hard to fight — alternating between conciliation and suppression without fully committing to either. Fourth, the logistics of fighting a war 3,000 miles from home were unsustainable indefinitely. Fifth, at Yorktown specifically, de Grasse arrived and Graves arrived late. The Revolution was won by endurance, alliance, British indecision, and six days.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Five factors that won the American Revolution — French alliance, Washington's patience, British indecision, logistical overextension, and the six-day gap between de Grasse and Graves. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Declaration and the Constitution — What Yorktown Protected
scenes.narration: The Declaration of Independence stated the principles. The Revolution fought for them. Yorktown secured them. The Constitution of 1787 institutionalized them. What emerged from this sequence was a set of ideas that spread across the world over the following two centuries: that all people possess inherent rights, that government exists to protect those rights, that when government fails this purpose the people may alter or abolish it. These ideas were not perfectly realized in 1781 — slavery continued for 84 more years, women couldn't vote for 139 more years. But the framework that eventually corrected these failures was built at Yorktown and Philadelphia. The tree planted in 1781 is still growing.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Declaration, the Revolution, Yorktown, the Constitution — the sequence that created the framework for rights-based government, the imperfect but expanding arc of American democracy. 16:9 cinematic.
